About Me

Hi! I'm Aarzoo Mann, a data analyst with a passion for uncovering patterns that drive business outcomes. I'm developing skills in statistical modeling, data storytelling and building dashboards that people actually use.

My work spans exploratory data analysis, predictive modeling and visualization. I'm equally comfortable writing a regression model in R, automating pipelines in Python or crafting an executive dashboard in Power BI.

When I'm not wrangling data, I'm probably obsessing over data visualization designs or how to forecast the FIFA World Cup '26 winner.

Interactive Data Viz

Live demos of the kind of visualizations I build — click the charts to interact.

0.82
R²
0.82
R² Adjusted
6.93
S (Std. Error)
ŷ = 39.1 + 2.78x
Fitted Line
The Story Behind the Numbers

I wanted to see if something we all kind of already know was actually backed by data — "Does studying more genuinely lead to better scores?". Using a dataset of 62 undergraduate students tracking their weekly study hours and corresponding assignment scores, I ran a simple linear regression. The fitted line came out as Score = 39.1 + 2.78 × Study Hours, which means for every extra hour of studying per week, a student's score goes up by about 2.8 points on average. The model explains 82% of the variation in scores (R² = 0.82), which is honestly pretty strong for real-world data. The standard error of 6.93 tells us our predictions stay within roughly ±7 points which is decent, but it also means other factors like attendance or just raw ability still play a role. So studying more does move the needle, but it's not the whole picture (see mom! I told you.)

0.34
R²
0.32
R² Adjusted
9.17
S (Std. Error)
ŷ = 101.3 − 4.6x
Fitted Line
The Story Behind the Numbers

With the Spotify Top 50 dataset already in hand (27,800+ records across daily charts), I got curious about something pretty simple: does the length of a song have anything to do with how popular it gets? Using song duration (in minutes) as the predictor and Spotify's popularity score (0–100) as the outcome, the regression line came out as Popularity = 101.3 − 4.6 × Duration. That means every extra minute a song runs is associated with a drop of about 4.6 points in popularity, which honestly makes sense in the streaming era, where shorter, hook-heavy tracks tend to rack up more plays. The R² of 0.34 tells us that duration explains about a third of the variation in popularity. Artists, genre and release timing all still matter a lot, but if you're making a song to chart, maybe don't make it a 6-minute epic.

Python
scikit-learn

House Price Prediction using Regression

A hands-on Python project applying both Simple and Multiple Linear Regression on a hypothetical housing dataset to predict prices in lakh rupees. Built using sklearn from scratch through data prep, model training, coefficient extraction, residual analysis and visualisation.

0.98
R² Score
4.0
Mean Sq. Error
0.04
Slope (β₁)
1.0
Intercept (β₀)
Live Regression Plot — Area vs Price
Fitted line: Price = 1.0 + 0.04 × Area  ·  Blue dots = actual data  ·  Red line = model prediction

Residual Analysis

Residuals were small and centred around zero, confirming a good model fit with no systematic bias.

RESIDUALS OUTPUT
0−1.0 1−1.0 2+4.0 3−1.0 4−1.0

Part 1 — Simple Linear Regression

Predicting house price from area alone (sq. ft → lakh rupees).

import numpy as np import pandas as pd import matplotlib.pyplot as plt from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression from sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error, r2_score # Sample dataset: area in sq.ft, price in lakh rupees data = {'Area': [500,750,1000,1250,1500], 'Price': [20,30,45,50,60]} df = pd.DataFrame(data) x = df['Area'].values.reshape(-1, 1) y = df['Price'] # Model Training model = LinearRegression() model.fit(x, y) # Intercept(B1): 0.999 | Slope(B2): 0.04 # Prediction & Residuals prediction = model.predict(x) residual = y - prediction # Residuals: [−1, −1, +4, −1, −1] # Metrics print('MSE:', mean_squared_error(y, prediction)) # → 4.0 print('R²:', r2_score(y, prediction)) # → 0.9804

Part 2 — Multiple Linear Regression

Extended the model to include Area, Bedrooms, and Age as predictors — mimicking a real-world scenario where multiple factors drive price.

# Updated dataset with multiple features data = {'Area': [500,750,1000,1250,1500], 'Bedrooms': [2,3,2,4,3], 'Age': [10,5,8,4,2], 'Price': [20,30,45,50,60]} df_multi = pd.DataFrame(data) model = LinearRegression() model.fit(df_multi[['Area','Bedrooms','Age']], df_multi['Price']) # Intercept: −5.577 # Coefficients: [0.0846, −1.039, 0.654] # Predicted prices: [21.73, 28.85, 43.27, 50.00, 61.15] predicted = model.predict(df_multi[['Area','Bedrooms','Age']])

Multiple Regression Coefficients

Feature

Interpretation
Intercept

+0.654

Python
sklearn
200K+ Records

Diamond Price Prediction using Regression

Leveraged 200,000+ diamond records to build a robust predictive model using both Simple and Multiple Linear Regression. The project explores how physical and quality attributes — carat, cut, colour, and clarity — drive diamond prices, progressing from a single-variable model to a full multivariate pipeline.

0.85
R² (Simple)
0.92
R² (Multiple)
7,756
Slope β (Carat)
200K+
Records
Regression Plot — Carat vs Price (USD)
Fitted line: Price = −2,256 + 7,756 × Carat  ·  Each point = a diamond  ·  Amber line = model

Feature Correlation Heatmap

Graphical representation that visually displays the correlation matrix of features in a dataset. It is used to identify highly correlated features, which can then be considered for removal or further analysis during the feature selection process.

−1.0 (negative)
+1.0 (positive)

Part 1 — Simple Linear Regression (Carat → Price)

Started with the strongest single predictor: carat weight. Even with one variable, the model explains 85% of price variation, confirming carat is by far the dominant driver.

import pandas as pd import numpy as np import matplotlib.pyplot as plt from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression from sklearn.metrics import r2_score, mean_squared_error # Load diamonds dataset (200,000+ records) df = pd.read_csv('diamonds.csv') print(df.shape) # (200000, 10) # Simple regression: Carat → Price X = df[['carat']] y = df['price'] model = LinearRegression() model.fit(X, y) print('Intercept:', model.intercept_) # → −2256.36 print('Slope:', model.coef_[0]) # → 7756.43 y_pred = model.predict(X) print('R²:', r2_score(y, y_pred)) # → 0.8493 print('RMSE:', np.sqrt(mean_squared_error(y, y_pred))) # → 1548.7 # Visualisation plt.scatter(X, y, alpha=0.1, color='steelblue', label='Actual') plt.plot(X, y_pred, color='red', label='Regression Line') plt.xlabel('Carat'); plt.ylabel('Price (USD)') plt.title('Diamond Price Prediction — Simple Linear Regression') plt.legend(); plt.show()

Part 2 — Multiple Linear Regression

Extended the model to include carat, depth, table, length, width and depth dimensions, plus encoded cut, colour and clarity which pushed R² to 0.92.

from sklearn.preprocessing import LabelEncoder from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split # Encode categorical features le = LabelEncoder() for col in ['cut', 'color', 'clarity']: df[col] = le.fit_transform(df[col]) features = ['carat','cut','color','clarity','depth','table','x','y','z'] X_multi = df[features] y_multi = df['price'] X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split( X_multi, y_multi, test_size=0.2, random_state=42) model_multi = LinearRegression() model_multi.fit(X_train, y_train) y_pred_multi = model_multi.predict(X_test) print('R² (Multiple):', r2_score(y_test, y_pred_multi)) # → 0.9197 print('RMSE:', np.sqrt(mean_squared_error(y_test, y_pred_multi))) # → 1124.4

Key Feature Coefficients

Feature
Coefficient
Effect on Price
Carat
+8,886
Strongest driver by far
Clarity
+545
Higher clarity → higher price
Cut
+278
Better cut grades add value
Depth
−63
Slight negative (non-linear)
Color
−214
Lower colour grade → lower price
